framework.reasoning: This paper is a stochastic-control/probability-theory paper, not a neural-network paper, so there is no model to 'train' in the conventional sense. The bulk of the repo is NumPy/SciPy scientific computing (matrix ODEs/Riccati paths, closed-form martingale depth formulas, Kalman-Bucy filtering, Monte Carlo path simulation). PyTorch is selected as the nominal 'primary' framework (schema requires one of pytorch/jax/tensorflow) solely because it is used, optionally, for the exploratory deep-BSDE-style numerical solver referenced in implementation_assumptions for Section 5.5/5.7 (autodiff is convenient there); torch.Tensor is not required anywhere else and every core module is pure NumPy so it also runs with zero GPU/deep-learning dependencies installed.

module_hierarchy.description: YAML config loading + global seeding (numpy, python random, torch if available).

Numerical illustration of the matrix-valued Doob-Meyer decomposition (Appendix B, Theorem B.3): decomposes a simulated matrix submartingale path into local-martingale + nondecreasing parts via the e_i+e_j coordinate trick.

public_methods.name: decompose
public_methods.returns: (M_paths, A_paths): local-martingale and nondecreasing parts, same shape as X_paths [T, n, n]

module_hierarchy.description: Primal covariance-liquidation solver and dual martingale-depth solver for all benchmark cases in Section 5: constant sigma (Kyle 1985), deterministic sigma(t) (Back-Pedersen 1998), deterministic-growth stochastic sigma (Collin-Dufresne-Fos 2016), and the common-eigenbasis multi-asset case (Back-Cocquemas-Ekren-Lioui 2020).

public_methods.name: M_star
public_methods.returns: M*_t along a simulated sigma path, eq. (5.22)/(5.27)

public_methods.name: M_star
public_methods.signature: (t: np.ndarray, V: np.ndarray, scalar_solvers: list) -> np.ndarray

module_hierarchy.description: Kalman-Bucy price filter: simulates order flow Y_t, price P_t = E[v|F^M_t], and posterior covariance Sigma_t given a depth process M_t.

public_methods.name: simulate
public_methods.signature: (depth_model, v_true: np.ndarray, C: np.ndarray, T: float, n_steps: int, seed: int) -> dict
public_methods.returns: dict with keys P (price path), Sigma (covariance path), Y (order flow), X (insider position), t (time grid)

module_hierarchy.description: Insider's equilibrium optimal trading strategy dX*_t (eq. 3.8) and inconspicuousness check (eq. 3.12).

Runs all Section 5 benchmark verifications and checks the terminal covariance identity (eq. 4.6), MDC's interior-martingale property (numerically: M_t stays uniformly positive-definite), and inconspicuousness (eq. 3.12). Produces the numbers consumed by Stage 6's comparator.

public_methods.name: run_all
public_methods.returns: dict of per-case verification records (closed_form_value, simulated_value, abs_error)

module_hierarchy.description: STUB for the general matrix-valued case (Section 5.7). The paper leaves wellposedness of the coupled matrix FBSDE (5.50)-(5.51) as an open problem, so this module only exposes a clearly-labeled, unverified heuristic fixed-point iteration and must never be treated as a validated result.

public_methods.name: solve_heuristic
public_methods.signature: (C: np.ndarray, sigma_path_fn, T: float, n_steps: int, n_iter: int) -> dict
public_methods.returns: STUB: unverified Picard-iteration approximation; raises a UserWarning on every call

module_hierarchy.description: Numerical comparison helpers: absolute/relative error between simulated depth process and paper's closed-form target, terminal-covariance-identity residual.

tensor_flows.name: Scalar Kyle (1985) equilibrium path
steps: simulate dY_t = dX*_t dt + sigma dB_t via Euler-Maruyama
P*_t = p0 + (sqrt(Sigma_0)/sigma) * Y_t  (eq. from D.1 specialization)
assert Sigma*_T ~ 0 and P*_T ~ v_true (terminal revelation, eq. 3.11)

tensor_flows.name: Common-eigenbasis multi-asset equilibrium path

input.name: C, sigma (n x n SPD, sharing eigenbasis V), T

output.name: M*_t, Sigma*_t (n x n paths), P*_t, X*_t (n-vectors)

steps: eigendecompose C and sigma jointly: C = V diag(Sigma0_i) V^T, sigma = V diag(sigma_i) V^T
solve n independent scalar Kyle problems, one per eigen-direction i
simulate n-dimensional order flow Y_t and verify M*_t matches the constant-volatility closed form (Section 5.3) when sigma is constant

model.n_assets: 1  # scalar benchmark by default; set 2 or 3 for common-eigenbasis demo
model.sigma_mode: constant  # one of: constant, deterministic_time, cdf_stochastic, common_eigenbasis

training.note: No training loop exists in this repo (no neural network is being fit). This section is kept only for schema compatibility with the ArXivist pipeline.
training.n_steps: 1000  # ASSUMED: time-discretization resolution for Euler-Maruyama SDE simulation, not specified in the paper

data.note: No dataset exists for this paper; all inputs (C, sigma) are synthetic parameters chosen by the user/config.

evaluation.closed_form_tolerance: 1e-6  # ASSUMED: numerical tolerance for asserting simulated depth matches closed-form target

runtime.reason: matrix square roots (scipy.linalg.sqrtm) for eq. (5.4),(5.11),(5.22),(C.3); eigendecomposition
plotting simulated vs closed-form paths in the notebook
OPTIONAL: only used by the explicitly-labeled fbsde_stub.py exploratory heuristic (Section 5.7); no other module imports torch

entrypoints.purpose: Replaces the conventional 'train.py' — this paper has no model to train. Runs the full Section-5 verification suite (simulate depth/price/covariance paths for every benchmark case and compare to closed-form formulas) and writes results to outputs/.

cli_args.help: Which benchmark case to run: kyle1985 | back_pedersen1998 | cdf2016 | common_eigenbasis | all
Reduce n_steps for a fast smoke test
Build all components, print shapes, but skip full simulation

entrypoints.purpose: Replaces 'evaluate.py'. Loads outputs/ from run_verification.py and produces the Stage-6-style comparison against the paper's closed-form targets.
Given a single (C, sigma, v_true) input, compute the equilibrium price/depth/strategy for one path (single-sample analog of 'inference').

risk_assessment.description: Section 5.7's fully general (non-commuting) matrix case has no known wellposedness result for its FBSDE; any code here is an unverified heuristic, not a reproduction.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implemented as fbsde_stub.py, clearly labeled STUB, raises UserWarning on every call, excluded from the default verification suite (only runs with an explicit --case flag).

risk_assessment.description: MDC (Definition 3.1) has no general numerical certificate; we can only confirm it empirically (M_t stays bounded away from the boundary of S^n_++ along a simulated path), which is not a proof.
risk_assessment.mitigation: verification.py reports the minimum eigenvalue of M_t along each simulated path as an empirical MDC health-check, explicitly labeled as non-rigorous.

risk_assessment.description: No numerical BSDE solver is specified in the paper for the scalar stochastic-liquidity case (Section 5.5); a full deep-BSDE or LSMC implementation would be substantial extra scope.
risk_assessment.mitigation: The default verification suite covers only the closed-form cases (5.1, 5.2, 5.4, 5.6) where M*_t is known in closed form; Section 5.5's general scalar stochastic-liquidity case is documented but not implemented, with a README note pointing to future work.

risk_assessment.description: Euler-Maruyama time discretization near t=T can make Sigma*_t numerically singular (it is exactly 0 at T by construction).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Simulation grid stops at T - dt/2 and clips any eigenvalue below 1e-10 before inversion, with a printed warning.

risk_assessment.description: No reported numerical results table exists in the paper (evaluation_protocol.reported_results is empty), so Stage 6's usual 'user results vs paper table' comparison does not apply.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Stage 6 is adapted to compare the repo's own simulated outputs against the paper's closed-form formulas (self-verification), which is the only meaningful notion of 'reproducibility' for a theory paper.
